blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلشَّمْسِ وَضُحَىٰهَا (١)
By the sun and her forenoon splendour, (1)
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا تَلَىٰهَا (٢)
by the moon when he follows her, (2)
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا جَلَّىٰهَا (٣)
by the day when it reveals her, (3)
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰهَا (٤)
by the night when it covers her, (4)
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا بَنَىٰهَا (٥)
by the sky and Him who built it, (5)
وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا طَحَىٰهَا (٦)
by the earth and Him who spread it, (6)
وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّىٰهَا (٧)
by the soul and Him who fashioned it, (7)
فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَىٰهَا (٨)
and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: (8)
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن زَكَّىٰهَا (٩)
one who purifies it is felicitous, (9)
وَقَدْ خَابَ مَن دَسَّىٰهَا (١٠)
and one who betrays it fails. (10)

Content and Virtue of Surah Ash-Shams

This Surah, which is in fact the Surah of "Self-Purification" and "Cleansing the Hearts from Impurities and Insincerities," revolves around this very theme. However, at the beginning of the Surah, eleven important subjects from the world of creation and the Holy Essence of Allah are sworn upon to prove that success and salvation depend on self-purification. It contains the greatest number of collective oaths in the Quran.

At the end of the Surah, it mentions an example of a rebellious and headstrong people who fell into eternal wretchedness due to their abandonment of self-purification, and whom Allah afflicted with a severe punishment—namely, the people of "Thamud"—and ends the Surah with a brief reference to their fate.

In truth, this short Surah recounts one of the most important fate-determining issues of human life and defines the Islamic value system regarding human beings.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, it is sufficient to mention a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites it, it is as if he has given charity of everything upon which the sun and the moon shine!" ^1 Certainly, this great virtue belongs to the one who implements the grand content of this small Surah within his soul and considers self-purification his definitive duty.

^1: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 496.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

وَ الشَّمْسِ وَ ضُحاها (1) وَ الْقَمَرِ إِذا تَلاها (2) وَ النَّهارِ إِذا جَلاَّها (3) وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشاها (4)

وَ السَّماءِ وَ ما بَناها (5) وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما طَحاها (6) وَ نَفْسٍ وَ ما سَوَّاها (7) فَأَلْهَمَها fُجُورَها وَ تَقْواها (8) قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها (9)

وَ قَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها (10)

Commentary

Salvation is Impossible Without Self-Purification

The consecutive and significant oaths appearing at the beginning of this Surah amount to "eleven" oaths by one count and "seven" by another. They represent the highest number of oaths in the Quran and clearly demonstrate that an important matter is being raised here—a matter as grand as the heavens, the earth, the sun, and the moon; a matter that is fate-determining and life-giving.

First, we must address the explanation and commentary of these oaths, and then examine that highly important subject which is the reason for all these proclamations.

First, He says: "By the sun and its spreading light" (وَ الشَّمْسِ وَ ضُحاها).

As we have stated previously, the oaths of the Quran generally pursue two objectives: first, the importance of the matter for which the oath is taken, and second, the importance of the things sworn by themselves. This is because an oath is always taken by important subjects; for this reason, these oaths stimulate human thought to reflect upon these significant subjects of the world of creation and to open a path through them toward Allah.

The "Sun" plays the most important and constructive role in the life of man and all living earthly beings. In addition to being the source of "light" and "heat"—which are two primary factors for human life—other vital resources also stem from it. The blowing of winds, the falling of rains, the growth of plants, the movement of rivers and waterfalls, and even the emergence of energy sources such as oil and coal, if we look closely, are each related to sunlight in some way. If this life-giving lamp were to be extinguished one day, darkness, silence, and death would envelop everything.

"Duha" originally means the spreading of sunlight, and this occurs when the sun rises above the horizon and its light covers everywhere. Subsequently, that time of day is also called "Duha."

The emphasis specifically on "Duha" is due to its importance, as it is the time of the sunlight's dominance over the earth.

Then, turning to the third oath, He says: "And by the moon when it follows it" (وَ الْقَمَرِ إِذا تَلاها).

This expression—as a group of commentators has stated—is actually a reference to the moon at the time of the full moon (the fourteenth night). This is because on the fourteenth night, the moon rises from the eastern horizon almost at sunset, revealing its radiant face and establishing its dominance over the expanse of the sky. Since it is more beautiful and magnificent at this time than any other, it is sworn by.

The possibility has also been raised that the above expression refers to the moon's constant following of the sun and its acquisition of light from that source, but in this case, the phrase "إِذا تَلاها" would be an explanatory clause.

Some other possibilities have been suggested in the commentary of the verse which are not noteworthy, so we refrain from mentioning them.

And in the fourth oath, He adds: "And by the day when it brightens the surface of the earth" (وَ النَّهارِ إِذا جَلَّاها). "Jallaha" is derived from the root "Tajliyah," meaning to manifest and reveal.

There is discussion among commentators regarding what the pronoun in "Jallaha" refers to. Many return it to the earth or the world (as we mentioned above). It is true that "earth" was not mentioned in the preceding verses, but it is clear from the context.

Some also believe the pronoun refers to the "sun," meaning: by the "day" when it makes the "sun" manifest. It is true that in reality, the sun makes the day manifest, but it can be said metaphorically that the day revealed the sun. However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In any case, the oath by this important celestial phenomenon is due to its extraordinary impact on human life and all living beings, for the day is the symbol of movement, activity, and life, and all efforts, strivings, and endeavors of life usually take place in the light of day.

And in the fifth oath, He says: "By the night when it covers the surface of the earth (or the sun)" (وَ اللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشاها) ^1. Night, with all its blessings and effects, on one hand moderates the heat of the daytime sun, and on the other hand, is the source of peace and rest for all living beings. If the darkness of night did not exist and the sun shone continuously, there would be no peace, for the scorching heat of the sun would destroy everything. Even if the system of night and day were different from its current state, the same problem would arise. For instance, on the moon, where nights are equivalent to two weeks on Earth and days are also equivalent to two weeks, the temperature in the middle of the day reaches about three hundred degrees Celsius—conditions in which no living being known to us could survive—and in the middle of the night, it drops significantly below zero, so that if any living being were there, it would certainly freeze and perish.

It is noteworthy that in the previous verses, the verbs were in the "past" tense, while in this verse, it is in the "present/future" (Mudari') tense. This difference in expression may indicate that events such as the appearance of day and night are not specific to one time; they encompass the past and the future. Thus, some appear as past tense and some as present tense to clarify the universality of these events across the span of time.

In the sixth and seventh oaths, He turns to the heaven and the Creator of the heaven, adding: "By the heaven and Him Who built it" (وَ السَّماءِ وَ ما بَناها). The original creation of the heaven with its staggering grandeur is among the great wonders of creation, and the building and emergence of all these stars, celestial bodies, and the systems governing them is another wonder, and more important than that is the Creator of this heaven.

It is noteworthy that "Ma" in the Arabic language is usually used for "non-rational beings," and its application to the All-Knowing, All-Wise Allah might seem unsuitable. Therefore, some have felt compelled to take "Ma" here as "Masdariyah" (infinitival) rather than "Mawsulah" (relative), in which case the meaning of the verse would be: "By the heaven and the building of the heaven."

However, considering the verses "وَ نَفْسٍ وَ ما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَ تَقْواها," the commentary of which will soon be explained, there is no way but to consider "Ma" as "Mawsulah" and a reference to the Holy Essence of Allah, Who is the Creator of all the heavens. The use of "Ma" for rational beings is not unprecedented in the Arabic language, as we read in Surah An-Nisa, Verse 3: "فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ" (Marry those women who seem good to you).

A group of commentators believes that the use of "Ma" (what/that which) here is to first mention the Origin of the universe in an ambiguous form, so that later, through careful study, one becomes acquainted with His knowledge and wisdom, and "something" turns into "Someone." However, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

Then, in the "eighth" and "ninth" oaths, He speaks of the "earth" and the "Creator of the earth," saying: "By the earth and Him Who spread it" (وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما طَحاها). The earth, which is the cradle of life for man and all living beings.

The earth with all its wonders: mountains and seas, valleys and forests, springs and rivers, its precious minerals and resources—each of which is alone a sign among the signs of Truth and a mark among His marks.

And greater and higher than that is the Creator of this earth and the One Who spread it.

"Tahaha" is derived from the root "Tahw" (rhyming with Sahw), which means both expansion and spreading, as well as "driving, pushing away, and removing." Here it means spreading, because firstly, the earth was initially submerged under water; gradually the waters settled in the hollows of the earth, and the dry lands emerged and spread—this is also referred to as "Dahw al-Ard."

Secondly, the earth was initially composed of heights and depths with steep slopes unsuitable for habitation. Continuous torrential rains fell, washing away the elevations of the earth and spreading them into the valleys, and gradually flat lands usable for human life and cultivation were formed.

Some commentators believe that in this expression, there is also a brief allusion to the movement of the earth, because one of the meanings of "Tahw" is "driving," which could refer to the earth's orbital motion around the sun, or even its rotational motion around itself, or both.

Finally, He turns to the "tenth" and "eleventh" oaths, which are the last in this series, saying: "By the soul of man, and Him Who proportioned and ordered it" (وَ نَفْسٍ وَ ما سَوَّاها). That same human being who is the essence of the world of creation, the distillation of the realm of the physical and the spiritual, and the crown of the world of existence.

This marvelous creation, which is full of wonders and secrets, is so important that Allah has sworn by Himself and its Creator together!

Regarding whether "Nafs" (soul/self) here refers to the human spirit, or both the body and spirit, commentators have offered various possibilities.

If the spirit is intended, then "Sawwaha" (from the root Taswiyah) refers to the organization and balancing of the human spiritual faculties, ranging from the external senses to the power of perception, memory, transition, imagination, innovation, love, will, and decision-making, as discussed in "Psychology" (Ilm al-Nafs).

And if both "spirit" and "body" are intended, it includes all the wonders of the body's systems and its various organs, which are extensively discussed in the sciences of "Anatomy" and "Physiology."

Of course, "Nafs" is used in both senses in the Holy Quran:

Regarding the "spirit," we read in Az-Zumar, Verse 42: "اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها" (Allah takes the souls at the time of their death).

And regarding the "body," it appears in Al-Qasas, Verse 33, where Moses says: "قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي قَتَلْتُ مِنْهُمْ نَفْساً فَأَخافُ أَنْ يَقْتُلُونِ" (Moses said: My Lord, I have killed one of them [from the oppressive Pharaohs], so I fear they will kill me).

However, it is appropriate here to include both, for the wonders of Allah's power are in both the body and the soul and are not restricted to one of the two. Interestingly, "Nafs" is mentioned here in the indefinite form, which can point to the greatness and importance of the human soul—a greatness beyond imagination and mixed with ambiguity that introduces it as an unknown being, just as some of the greatest modern scientists have referred to man by this title, calling him "Man, the Unknown."

In the next verse, He addresses one of the most important issues related to human creation, adding: "After organizing the faculties and the body and soul of man, He inspired it with its 'Fujur' (wickedness) and its 'Taqwa' (piety)" (فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَ تَقْواها). Yes, when his creation was completed and his "existence" was realized, Allah taught him the "dos and don'ts." Thus, he became a being who, in terms of creation, is a combination of "foul-smelling clay" and "Divine spirit," and in terms of teachings, is "aware of wickedness and piety." Consequently, he is a being who can, in the ascending arc, become higher than the angels, fly beyond the celestial realm, and become that which cannot be imagined; and in the descending arc, become more debased than predatory animals and reach the stage of "بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ" (rather, they are further astray). This depends on which path he chooses with his will and power of choice.

"Alhamaha" is from the root "Ilham," which originally means swallowing or drinking something, and subsequently came to mean the casting of a matter by the Lord into the soul and heart of man, as if the human soul drinks and swallows that matter with its entire being. Sometimes it also means revelation (Wahy), but some commentators believe the difference between "Ilham" and "Wahy" is that the person receiving Ilham does not understand where the matter came from, whereas during Wahy, he knows from where and by what means it reached him.

"Fujur" is from the root "Fajr," as we have previously noted, meaning a wide splitting. Since the dawn breaks the veil of night, it is called "Fajr." Also, since committing sins tears the veil of religiosity, it is called "Fujur."

Of course, "Fujur" in the verse under discussion refers to its causes, factors, and paths.

And "Taqwa," which is from the root "Wiqayah" meaning protection, means that man protects and keeps himself away from ugliness, evils, impurities, and sins.

It is necessary to remind that the meaning of this verse (فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَ تَقْواها) is not that Allah created the factors of wickedness and piety within the human soul—factors that invite him to wickedness, impurity, and the tearing of the veils of modesty, and factors that draw him toward goodness and virtues—as some have imagined, using the verse as evidence for the existence of contradiction within human existence.

Rather, it says He inspired and taught him these two truths, or in simpler terms, He showed him the path and the pit, just as it appears in Surah Al-Balad, Verse 10: "وَ هَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ" (And We showed him the two ways [of good and evil]).

In other words: Allah has given him such power of discernment, intellect, and an awake conscience that he perceives "wickedness" and "piety" through "reason" and "nature" (Fitrah).

Therefore, some commentators have said this verse actually refers to the issue of "rational beauty and ugliness" (al-husn wal-qubh al-aqli), the ability to perceive which Allah has given to human beings.

It is noteworthy that Allah has placed many blessings at the disposal of man, but among all these blessings, He emphasizes here the issue of the inspiration of "wickedness" and "piety" and the perception of beauty and ugliness, because this issue is the most fate-determining of all matters in human life.

And finally, after all these important and consecutive oaths, He turns to their result, saying: "By these, he who purifies his soul has succeeded" (قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها). "Zakkaha" is from the root "Tazkiyah," which originally—as Raghib has noted in Al-Mufradat—means to grow and develop. Zakat also originally means growth and development; hence, we read in a narration from Ali عليه السلام:

"Wealth is decreased by spending, but knowledge grows (yazku) upon spending." ^1 Subsequently, this word also came to mean purification and cleansing, perhaps because cleansing from impurities is a cause for growth and development. In the verse under discussion, both meanings are possible.

Yes, success belongs to the one who trains his soul and makes it grow and develop, and cleanses it from contamination by satanic character, sin, rebellion, and disbelief. In truth, the primary issue of human life is this very "purification," which if present, makes one happy, and otherwise, wretched and miserable.

Then He turns to the opposing group, saying: "And he who contaminated his soul with transgression and sin has failed and been deprived" (وَ قَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها). "Khab" is from the root "Khaybah," meaning failing to reach the goal, being deprived, and becoming a loser. ^1 "Dassaha" is from the root "Dass," which originally means inserting something with reluctance, as the Holy Quran says regarding the pre-Islamic Arabs and the burying of daughters alive: "أَمْ يَدُسُّهُ فِي التُّرابِ" (or bury it in the dust with reluctance and hatred) (An-Nahl: 59). "Dasisa" (intrigue/conspiracy) is used for harmful secret actions.

Various explanations have been given regarding the suitability of this meaning for the verse under discussion:

Sometimes it is said this expression is a metaphor for immorality and sin, because the people of piety and righteousness manifest themselves, while contaminated and sinful individuals hide themselves. As it is narrated, the generous among the Arabs would pitch their tents on high ground and light fires at night so that the needy could find them day and night and receive their kindness, but the stingy and mean would pitch tents in low-lying lands so that no one would come to them!

Sometimes it is said the meaning is that sinners hide themselves among the righteous.

Or that they hide their soul or their human identity in transgressions and sin.

Or they hide transgressions and sin within their soul.

In any case, it is a metaphor for contamination with sin, transgression, and satanic traits, and is exactly the opposite of purification.

There is no obstacle to combining all these meanings within the broad concept of the verse.

In this way, the victors and the defeated on the stage of worldly life are identified, and the criterion for evaluating these two groups is nothing other than "purification of the soul and the growth and development of the spirit of piety and obedience to Allah" or "contamination with various transgressions and sins."

From here it becomes clear that what was narrated in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليهما السلام in the commentary of the verse:

"He who obeyed succeeded, and he who rebelled failed and was deprived" ^1 is in fact an explanation of the result and the intended purpose.

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, when reciting the verse "قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها," would pause and pray thus:

"O Allah, grant my soul its piety; You are its Guardian and its Master, and purify it, for You are the best of those who purify." [^2] This statement shows that traversing this winding path and passing this difficult mountain pass is not possible even for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله except by Divine success—meaning with steps from the servants and support from Allah. Therefore, in another Hadith from him, we read that he said in the commentary of these two verses:

"The soul whom Allah purified succeeded, and the soul whom Allah deprived of every good failed and was deprived!" ^1

Key Points

1- The Connection Between the Quranic Oaths and Their Result

What relationship exists between these eleven very important oaths and the truth for which the oath was taken?

It seems the goal is the expression of this truth by the Great Allah: that I have provided all material and spiritual means for the success and happiness of you humans. On one hand, I have brightened and enriched the scene of your life with the light of the sun and moon, regulated your movement and rest with the system of day and night, and prepared the earth for your life in every respect.

On the other hand, I have created your soul with all necessary talents, given you an awake conscience, and inspired you with the beauty and ugliness of matters. Therefore, you have no deficiency in traversing the path of happiness. Despite this, how is it that you do not purify your soul?

And why do you surrender to satanic intrigues?

2- The Role of the Sun in the World of Life

Regarding the sun, which is the center of the solar system and the leader and master of its planets, there are two discussions: one regarding its grandeur, which we have spoken of before, and the other regarding its blessings and effects, which can be summarized as follows:

1- The life of man and all other living beings primarily requires heat and light, and these two vital matters are provided in a perfectly balanced manner by this great sphere of fire.

2- All food substances are provided by sunlight; even beings living in the depths of the seas use plants that grow on the surface of the oceans in the light of the sun and among the waves, and then settle down. Or if living beings feed on one another, the food of a group of them is from plants and vegetation that do not grow without sunlight.

3- All colors, beauties, and manifestations we see in the world of nature are somehow related to the shining of the sun, and this meaning has been proven in various sciences, especially physics.

4- Life-giving rains fall from the clouds, and clouds are those very vapors created by the sun's shining upon the surface of the oceans. Therefore, all water resources fed by rain—including streams, springs, qanats, and deep wells—are from the blessings of sunlight.

5- Winds, which are responsible for moderating the air, moving clouds, pollinating plants, and transferring heat and cold from hot to cold regions and vice versa, are created due to the sun's shining and the change in temperature of different regions on earth. Thus, they too stem from the sun.

6- Energy-producing materials and resources—including waterfalls, the great dams built in mountainous regions, oil resources, and coal mines—are all somehow tied to the sun. If it were not for the sun, none of these resources would exist; all movement on the surface of the earth would turn into stillness!

7- The survival of the solar system's order is due to the balance of attraction and repulsion between the sun on one hand and the planets revolving around it on the other. Thus, the sun plays a very effective role in keeping these planets in their orbits.

From the sum of this discussion, we understand why Allah began the first oath with the sun! Likewise, the moon, the brightness of day, the darkness of night, and the planet earth each play an important role in the life of humans and non-humans, and for this reason, they are sworn by. Beyond all these is the human soul and body, which are the most mysterious and wonderful of all.

We will have a discussion regarding the importance of self-purification at the end of the Surah.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْوَىٰهَآ (١١)
The [people of] Thamud denied [Allah’s signs] out of their rebellion, (11)
إِذِ ٱنۢبَعَثَ أَشْقَىٰهَا (١٢)
when the most wretched of them rose up. (12)
فَقَالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ نَاقَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَسُقْيَـٰهَا (١٣)
The apostle of Allah had told them, ‘This is Allah’s she-camel, let her drink!’ (13)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوهَا فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسَوَّىٰهَا (١٤)
But they impugned him and hamstrung her. So their Lord took them unawares by night because of their sin, and levelled it. (14)
وَلَا يَخَافُ عُقْبَـٰهَا (١٥)
And He does not fear its outcome. (15)

Commentary

The Deadly End of the Transgressors

Following the warnings in the previous verses regarding the fate of those who corrupt their souls, these verses present a clear historical example of this matter. Describing the destiny of the rebellious people of "Thamud" in brief, decisive, and meaningful terms, He says:

"The people of Thamud denied [their Prophet] because of their transgression, and rejected the Divine signs" (كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْواها).

"Taghwa" and "Tughyan" both carry the same meaning, which is to exceed limits and boundaries. Here, it refers to exceeding Divine limits and rebelling against His commands. [^1] The people of Thamud, whose Prophet was "Salih" (peace be upon him), were among the most ancient peoples who lived in a mountainous region between "Hijaz" and "Sham" (Syria). They enjoyed a luxurious life, a prosperous land, flat plains with soil ready for cultivation, magnificent palaces, and sturdy houses. However, not only did they fail to show gratitude for all these blessings, but they rose in rebellion, stood up to deny their Prophet Salih, mocked the Divine signs, and ultimately, Allah destroyed them with a heavenly thunderbolt.

[^1]: From the statements of some lexicographers, it is understood that "Tughyan" appears in both naqis-wawi and naqis-ya'i forms; "Taghwa" is derived from the naqis-wawi root and "Tughyan" from the naqis-ya'i (take note).

Then, pointing to one of the most prominent examples of this nation's rebellion, it adds:

"When the most wretched of them rose up" (إِذِ انْبَعَثَ أَشْقاها). "Ashqa" means the "most wretched" and "most hard-hearted" individual of that nation. This refers to the person who killed the She-camel of Thamud—the same She-camel that had appeared as a miracle among those people. Killing it was a declaration of war against that Divine Prophet.

According to commentators and historians, this person's name was "Qudar bin Salif."

In some narrations, it is mentioned that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام:

"Who was the most wretched of the former peoples?"

Ali عليه السلام replied: "The one who hamstrung the She-camel (the killer of the She-camel of Thamud)."

The Prophet said: "You have spoken the truth. Then who is the most wretched of the latter peoples?" Ali عليه السلام says: "I said: I do not know, O Messenger of Allah."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "The one who shall strike you upon this," and he pointed to his crown (the top of his head). "The one who will strike the sword upon this point of your head," and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله pointed to the upper part of his forehead. [^1] and [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 499; the same meaning is mentioned more briefly in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 7168. [^2]: "Yafukh" refers to that part of the front of the head which is completely soft in infants and gradually becomes bony and hard; it is one of the most sensitive points of the head.

In the next verse, providing more detail regarding the rebellion of the people of Thamud, it adds: "The Messenger of Allah said to them: [Leave] the She-camel of Allah and her drink, and do not interfere with it" (فَقالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ ناقَةَ اللَّهِ وَ سُقْياها). [^1] The "Messenger of Allah" here refers to Prophet Salih عليه السلام, the Prophet of the people of Thamud. The expression "Naqat-Allah" (the She-camel belonging to Allah) indicates that this was no ordinary camel, but was sent as a miracle and a living testament to the truth of Salih's claim. According to famous narrations, one of its characteristics was that it emerged from the heart of a rock in the mountain to be a clear miracle before the stubborn deniers.

From other verses of the Quran, it is well understood that Prophet Salih عليه السلام informed them that the village's drinking water must be divided between them and the She-camel: one day for the She-camel and another day for the people of the village, and each should use the water in their turn without interfering with the other: "And inform them that the water is shared between them, every [share of] drink [for each] being attended [in turn]" (Qamar: 28).

He specifically told them: If you harm this She-camel, the punishment of Allah will seize you: "And do not touch her with harm, lest the punishment of a great day seize you" (Shu'ara: 156).

And in the next verse, it says: This rebellious people paid no heed to the words of this great Prophet and his warnings; they denied him and killed the She-camel (فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوها).

[^1]: "Naqat-Allah" is in the accusative case (mansub) due to a hidden verb, and the estimation is: "Leave the She-camel of Allah and her drink," similar to what appears in Al-A'raf: 73 and Hud: 64.

"Aqaruha" is from the root "Aqr," which means the origin, foundation, or root of something. "Aqr" of the She-camel means to uproot and destroy it.

Some have also said it means to hamstring the animal—that is, cutting its lower legs to bring it to the ground, which also results in the animal's death.

It is noteworthy that the one who killed the She-camel was only one person, whom the Quran describes as "Ashqa" (the most wretched). However, in the above verse, we see this act is attributed to all the transgressors of the people of Thamud, and "Aqaruha" is used in the plural form. This is because others were also involved in this act in some way. Firstly, such conspiracies are usually planned by a group or community and then executed by a specific individual or a few people. Secondly, because it was done with the consent and satisfaction of the others, it makes them partners in the matter, as satisfaction leads to partnership in the outcome.

Therefore, we read in the eloquent and expressive words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

"Only one man hamstrung the She-camel of Thamud, but Allah encompassed them all in the punishment because they all encompassed it with their consent. Thus He, the Glorified, said: 'So they hamstrung her, and then they became regretful.'"

"The She-camel of Thamud was killed by only one person, but Allah included everyone in the punishment because they all consented to this act. Therefore, He said: They (all) killed the She-camel, and then they all became regretful of their deed (but at a time when regret was of no use)." [^1] Following this denial and intense opposition, Allah punished them such that no trace of them remained, as He says in the continuation of the same verse: "So their Lord crushed them for their sin and leveled them" (فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسَوَّاها).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 201.

The thunderbolt—that great heavenly cry—created such an earthquake and tremor in their land in a few short moments that all buildings collapsed and were leveled, turning their homes into their graves.

"Damdama" from the root "Damdamah" is sometimes used to mean to destroy, sometimes to mean complete punishment, sometimes to mean crushing and softening, sometimes to mean uprooting, and sometimes to mean expressing anger or encompassing and overwhelming. [^1] All these meanings are applicable in the verse under discussion, for this widespread punishment originated from Divine wrath and crushed, softened, and uprooted them all.

"Sawwaha" from the root "Taswiyah" may mean leveling their houses and lands due to the great cry, thunderbolt, and earthquake; or it may mean finalizing the matter for this group; or the equality of all of them in punishment such that not a single one of them escaped this event safely.

A combination of these meanings is also possible.

The pronoun in "Sawwaha" refers to the "tribe" of Thamud, or to their cities and settlements which Allah leveled with the ground.

Some have also said it refers to "Damdamah" (the destruction) mentioned in the preceding phrase, meaning Allah made this wrath and destruction equal among them so that it encompassed everyone.

The first interpretation seems more appropriate.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that their punishment was the result of their sin and proportionate to it, which is the essence of justice and wisdom.

Regarding many nations, we read that they became regretful upon the appearance of the signs of punishment and sought the path of repentance. But strangely, it is mentioned in some narrations that when the people of Salih saw the signs of punishment, they searched for him to destroy him wherever they might find him. [^1] This is evidence of the depth of their disobedience and rebellion against God and His Prophet. However, Allah saved him and destroyed them, completely folding up the scroll of their lives.

Finally, in the last verse, to give a firm warning to all those who move in the same path and direction, He says: "And He fears not the consequence thereof" (وَ لا يَخافُ عُقْباها).

There are many rulers who have the power to punish but are constantly fearful of its aftermath and terrified of reactions and repercussions. For this reason, they do not use their power, or more accurately, their power is mixed with weakness and their knowledge is mixed with ignorance, for they fear they may not have the ability to deal with the consequences.

But Allah, the All-Powerful, whose knowledge encompasses all these matters and their consequences and effects, and whose power to deal with the aftermath of events is not mixed with any weakness, has no fear of the consequences of these matters. For this reason, He performs what He wills with ultimate power and decisiveness.

Transgressors must also take account of their actions and beware of becoming subject to Divine wrath because of their deeds.

"Uqba" means the end, conclusion, and consequence of a matter, and the pronoun in "Uqbaha" refers to the "Damdamah" and destruction.

[^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, Lisan al-Arab, Majma' al-Bayan, and other books of Tafsir. [^1]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 446.

Key Points

1- Summary of the Story of the People of Thamud

The people of "Thamud," as we mentioned, lived in a land between Medina and Sham (called Wadi al-Qura). Their religion was idolatry, and they were immersed in various sins. Salih, the great Prophet of Allah, rose from among them and strove for their guidance and salvation, but they neither abandoned idolatry nor reconsidered their rebellion and sin.

When they requested a miracle, Allah brought forth a She-camel in a miraculous and extraordinary way from the heart of the mountain. However, to test them in this matter, He commanded that for one full day, the village's water should be at the disposal of this She-camel, and the next day it should be for themselves. It is even mentioned in some narrations that on the day they were deprived of water, they benefited from the She-camel's milk. Yet, even this great miracle did not diminish their stubbornness and enmity. They plotted both the destruction of the She-camel and the killing of Salih عليه السلام, as they saw him as an obstacle to their desires and whims.

The plan to destroy the She-camel, as mentioned, was carried out by a very cruel and wretched man named "Qudar bin Salif," who brought the She-camel down with strikes.

This was, in fact, a declaration of war against God, for by destroying the She-camel—which was Salih's miracle—they wanted to extinguish the light of guidance. It was then that Prophet Salih warned them that they could enjoy every blessing in their homes for three days, but they should know that after three days, Divine punishment would encompass them all (Surah Hud: 65).

These three days were a respite for a final reconsideration and the last opportunity for repentance and return. But not only did they not reconsider, they increased their rebellion. At this point, Divine punishment descended upon them. A heavenly cry [^1] crushed their land, and they all fell to the ground in their homes and died: "And the cry seized those who had wronged, and they became [lie] fallen prone in their homes" (Hud: 67).

They were so destroyed and their land became so silent that it was as if no one had ever inhabited that region. However, Allah saved Salih and his believing companions from this peril (Hud: 66).

For a more detailed account of the story of the people of Thamud, please refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 161 onwards.

[^1]: The heavenly cry, as we have previously noted, means a "thunderbolt," which possesses a great sound, creates a severe tremor, and is accompanied by fire and burning. Scientifically, it is a large electrical spark that occurs between clouds carrying a positive electrical charge and the earth which carries a negative charge.

2- "The Most Wretched of the Former Peoples" and "The Most Wretched of the Latter Peoples"

A group of great scholars of both the Shia and Sunni traditions, including "Thalabi," "Wahidi," "Ibn Mardawayh," "Khatib al-Baghdadi," "Tabari," "Mawsili," "Ahmad ibn Hanbal," and others, have narrated through their chains from "Ammar bin Yasir," "Jabir bin Samurah," and "Uthman bin Suhayb" from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said to Ali عليه السلام:

"O Ali! The most wretched of the former peoples was the killer of the She-camel, and the most wretched of the latter peoples is your killer."

And in a narration:

"The one who will dye this from this."

"O Ali! The most wretched of the predecessors was the one who killed the She-camel of Salih, and the most wretched individual of the latter ones is your killer." In one narration, it says: "The one who will dye this with that" (referring to the fact that he will dye your beard with the blood of your head). [^1] In reality, there was a similarity between "Qudar bin Salif," the one who hamstrung Salih's She-camel, and the killer of the Commander of the Faithful, "Abd al-Rahman bin Muljam al-Muradi." Neither of these two had a personal grudge; rather, both wanted to extinguish the light of truth and remove a miracle and a sign from the signs of Allah. Just as Divine punishment seized that rebellious and transgressing nation after the incident of Salih's She-camel, the Muslims also witnessed the most painful punishments under the rule of the tyrannical and oppressive Umayyad dynasty after the martyred Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام.

It is noteworthy that Hakim Haskani in Shawahid al-Tanzil has narrated numerous traditions in this regard that are similar in content and substance to the above narration. [^2]

3- Purification of the Soul: A Great Divine Duty

The more numerous and firm the Quranic oaths are regarding a subject, the more it indicates the importance of that topic. We know that the longest and most emphasized series of oaths is in this Surah, especially since the oath by the Pure Essence of Allah is repeated three times within it, and finally, emphasis is placed on the fact that success and salvation lie in the purification of the soul, while deprivation, failure, and misery lie in the abandonment of purification.

In fact, the most important issue of human life is this very matter. Indeed, the Quran expresses through the above phrasing that human salvation is not dependent on notions and imaginations, nor is it in the shadow of wealth, property, and status, nor is it tied to the actions of other persons (as Christians imagine that the salvation of every human is tied to the sacrifice of Jesus Christ), nor anything like these.

Rather, it is tied to the cleansing and elevation of the spirit and soul in the light of faith and righteous deeds.

Human misery and failure are neither due to forced predestination nor mandatory fates, nor are they the result of the activities of this person or that; rather, they are solely due to pollution with sin and deviation from the path of Taqwa (piety).

It is recorded in history that the wife of the Aziz of Egypt (Zulaikha), when Yusuf became the master of the treasures of the earth and the ruler of the land of Egypt, met him and said:

"Indeed, greed and lust turn kings into slaves, and indeed patience and Taqwa turn slaves into kings." Yusuf confirmed her words and reminded her of this Divine word: "Indeed, he who fears Allah and is patient, then indeed, Allah does not allow the reward of those who do good to be lost." [^1] This same meaning has been narrated in another way: that the wife of the Aziz of Egypt was sitting by the road when Yusuf's procession passed by. Zulaikha said: "Praise be to Allah who made kings into slaves because of their disobedience, and made slaves into kings because of their obedience." [^1] Yes, servitude to the self causes human slavery, and Taqwa and the purification of the soul cause sovereignty over the universe.

How many are those who, through servitude to Allah, have reached a station where they possess "generative authority" (wilayah takwini) and can, by Allah's permission, affect the events of this world and perform miracles and extraordinary feats.

O Allah! Be our helper and supporter in the struggle against the whims of the soul. O Lord! You inspired us with "wickedness" and "piety"; grant us the success to benefit from this inspiration. O God! The schemes of Satan are hidden and mysterious within the human soul; make us acquainted with the recognition of these schemes! Amen, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Al-Shams.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 587. [^2]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, pp. 335 to 343. [^1]: Mahajjat al-Bayda, Vol. 5, p. 116. [^1]: Ibid., p. 117.
